questions.scenario: The business says checkout must be usable again within 30 minutes after a serious database problem, and no more than about 5 minutes of accepted order writes can be lost.

options.text: 30 minutes is RTO, and 5 minutes of possible data loss is RPO.
30 minutes is RPO, and 5 minutes of possible data loss is RTO.
Both numbers are RTO because all recovery goals measure downtime.
Neither number matters once Azure SQL has backups.

questions.explanation: RTO is how long recovery can take. RPO is how fresh the recovered data must be, or how much recent data loss the business can tolerate.

questions.scenario: A nightly export can be rerun from source order records the next morning. Checkout cannot accept losing confirmed orders. What should the recovery plan do?

options.text: Give different promises to different workflows, because derived exports and source-of-truth orders do not need the same RTO/RPO.
Use the strictest checkout target for every derived file, regardless of cost.
Use the export target for checkout because batch jobs are cheaper.
Ignore RPO for source data because exports can be rebuilt.

questions.explanation: Recovery targets should belong to workflows. Source-of-truth data usually needs stronger protection than derived data that can be rebuilt.

questions.scenario: A proposed design has active compute in another region and replicated data. It costs much more than backup and restore. What question should decide whether it is worth it?

options.text: Does the workflow's business value require the shorter RTO/RPO and has the team tested the failover path?
Can the team make all systems active-active so every diagram looks consistent?
Can the team avoid writing down RTO and RPO because the design is expensive?
Can the team delete all backups after adding a second region?

questions.explanation: A stronger design can be justified for the right workflow, but cost, tested failover, and business impact must line up. Expense alone does not prove recovery works.

questions.scenario: Which statements use RTO or RPO correctly?

options.text: Checkout RTO can be shorter than admin analytics RTO.
Order records can have a stricter RPO than a search index that can be rebuilt.
Shorter RTO often costs more because it may need warm compute, tested routing, and on-call readiness.
RPO means how many app instances are running during normal traffic.

questions.explanation: RTO and RPO describe recovery promises. Normal replica count can support availability, but it is not the definition of RPO.
